title: Uniface Application Architecture
text: Uniface Application Architecture Uniface applications are based on a client/server architecture in which the user interface runs on the client and all other functionality runs on a server. The runtime engine executes the application components when it receives instructions to do so, either from the user or from other components. It ensures that presentation components are displayed to the user and sends and receives data via a DBMS driver. At its simplest, a Uniface application can run in standalone mode on a single machine that fills both client and server roles. Figure 1. Standalone Client/Server Application The Uniface runtime engine executes the application components. It ensures that presentation components are displayed to the user and sends and receives data via a DBMS driver. Uniface connectors (also known as drivers) enable Uniface to display a user interface, communicate over a network, access databases, and integrate with other technologies. These connectors give Uniface great flexibility in running on different platforms and in different environments. They also make it possible to use Uniface as an application integrator. The basic architecture can be varied in many ways because Uniface supports web, mobile, and desktop applications that can be deployed in a distributed environment, in which web servers, databases, and Uniface application servers may be located on different networked servers. The degree to which Uniface applications can be partitioned is dependent on the application design. For example, it is possible distribute an application so that forms run on the client and services run on a central server. For a web application, server pages are executed by a web application server that can communicate with services running in a Uniface application server. For more information, see Distributed Client/Server Applications and Uniface Web Applications . The Uniface connectors include: A user interface connector that provides a graphical user interface (GUI) or character-based user interface (CHUI) for the application. A network connector to access remote resources via TCP/IP. One or more DBMS connectors to access databases. Other connectors for accessing other technologies, such as COM, SOAP, and LDAP directories.
